Implements `strcat` and `strncat` functions for string concatenation.

# Purpose
This C source code file implements two string manipulation functions: [`strcat`](<#strcat>) and [`strncat`](<#strncat>). The [`strcat`](<#strcat>) function appends the string `s2` to the end of the string `s1`, assuming `s1` has enough space to hold the result. The [`strncat`](<#strncat>) function appends up to `n` characters from the string `s2` to the end of `s1`, ensuring that the result is null-terminated. Both functions return a pointer to the destination string `s1`. The code is optimized for minimal code size.
# Imports and Dependencies

---
- `string.h`


# Functions

---
### strcat<!-- {{#callable:strcat}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../../../../platform/tintin/boot/libc/string/strcat.c#L27>)

Concatenates the string `s2` to the end of the string `s1`.
- **Inputs**:
    - `s1`: A pointer to the destination string to which `s2` will be appended.
    - `s2`: A pointer to the source string that will be appended to `s1`.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Initialize a pointer `rc` to the start of `s1` to keep track of the original string.
    - Move the pointer `s1` to the end of the current string by adding the length of `s1` to it.
    - Copy the contents of `s2` to the end of `s1` using `strcpy`.
    - Return the pointer `rc` which points to the start of the concatenated string.
- **Output**: A pointer to the destination string `s1` after `s2` has been appended.


---
### strncat<!-- {{#callable:strncat}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../../../../platform/tintin/boot/libc/string/strcat.c#L34>)

Appends up to `n` characters from the string `s2` to the end of the string `s1`, ensuring `s1` is null-terminated.
- **Inputs**:
    - `s1`: A pointer to the destination string to which `s2` will be appended.
    - `s2`: A pointer to the source string from which characters will be appended to `s1`.
    - `n`: The maximum number of characters to append from `s2` to `s1`.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Initialize `rc` to point to `s1` for returning the original pointer.
    - Move `s1` to point to the null terminator of the current string in `s1`.
    - Calculate the length of `s2` and store it in `strn`.
    - If `strn` is greater than `n`, set `strn` to `n` to limit the number of characters to append.
    - Use `memcpy` to copy `strn` characters from `s2` to the end of `s1`.
    - Set the character at position `strn` in `s1` to '\0' to ensure the string is null-terminated.
    - Return the original pointer `rc`.
- **Output**: Returns a pointer to the destination string `s1`.
